title: An Analysis of Virtual Nodes in Graph Neural Networks for Link Prediction

paragraphs.text:
Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## REVISITING VIRTUAL NODES IN GRAPH NEURAL NETWORKS FOR LINK PREDICTION

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

It is well known that the graph classification performance of graph neural networks often improves by adding an artificial virtual node to the graphs, which is connected to all nodes in the graph. Intuitively, the virtual node provides a shortcut for message passing between nodes along the graph edges. Surprisingly, the impact of virtual nodes with other problems is still an open research question.

In this paper, we adapt the concept of virtual nodes to the link prediction scenario, where we usually have much larger, often dense, and more heterogeneous graphs. In particular, we use multiple virtual nodes per graph and graph-based clustering to determine the connections to the graph nodes. We also investigate alternative clustering approaches (e.g., random or more advanced) and compare to the original model with a single virtual node. We conducted extensive experiments over different datasets of the Open Graph Benchmark (OGB) and analyze the results in detail. We show that our virtual node extensions yield rather stable performance increases and allow standard graph neural networks to compete with complex state-of-the-art models, as well as with the models leading the OGB leaderboards.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

_Link prediction_ is an important task to complete graphs that are missing edges in various domains: citation networks (Kipf & Welling, 2016), social networks (Adamic & Adar, 2003), medical drug interaction graphs (Abbas et al., 2021), or knowledge graphs (KGs) (Ji et al., 2021). Numerous kinds of models have been proposed to solve the link prediction problem, ranging from KG-specific predictors (Ji et al., 2021) to graph neural networks (GNNs) (Kipf & Welling, 2016; Zhang & Chen, 2018). Over dense biomedical networks, GNNs turned out to work especially well (Hu et al., 2020).

In this work, we focus on _graph neural networks_ for link prediction. Many of the popular GNNs are based on the message-passing scheme, which computes node embeddings based on iteratively aggregating the features of (usually direct/one-hop) neighbor nodes along the graph edges (Gilmer et al., 2017). Interestingly, best performance is usually obtained by only considering two to three hops of neighbors (i.e., 2-3 layers in the GNN). One main reason identified for this is _over-smoothing_ , the problem that node representations become indistinguishable when the number of layers increases (Li et al., 2018). The exponentially-growing amount of information has also been suggested as one issue connected to capturing long-range dependencies (Alon & Yahav, 2021). While it is likely that link prediction most often depends on the local node neighborhood, it is not beyond imagination that there are critical long-range dependencies (e.g., complex chains of drug-drug or drug-protein interactions). Hence, using a small number of layers to overcome the above problems results in _under-reaching_ .

There have been several recent proposals to overcome under-reaching. On the one hand, several works propose techniques that allow for larger numbers of GNN layers (Xu et al., 2018; Wu et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2020; Chen et al., 2020; Sun et al., 2021; Zhou et al., 2020; Li et al., 2020a). However, although (Chen et al., 2020) show that over-smoothing happens particularly in dense graphs, the link prediction experiments in these works consider citation or recommendation networks, but not the especially dense biomedical ones. And our experiments over the latter suggest that the reported results are not generalizable to the more challenging biomedical data. On the other hand, there are approaches that adapt the message-passing scheme to consider neighbors beyond the one-hop neighborhood: based on graph diffusion (Atwood & Towsley, 2016; Klicpera et al., 2019a; Abu-El-Haija et al., 2019; Xu et al., 2019a; Ma et al., 2020; Klicpera et al., 2019b) and other theories (Morris et al., 2019; You

et al., 2019). However, most of these models are relatively complex and, in fact, in our experiments over the challenging graphs from the Open Graph Benchmark (OGB) (Hu et al., 2020), several ran out of memory. Moreover, the majority has not considered link prediction, while this problem was recently shown to be more difficult than node classification (Zhang et al., 2020).

In this paper, we propose a simple but elegant solution to under-reaching based on the concept of _virtual nodes_ (Gilmer et al., 2017; Li et al., 2017; Pham et al., 2017; Ishiguro et al., 2019). Virtual nodes are well known to often improve the graph classification performance of graph neural networks, where an artificial virtual node is added to every graph and connected to all nodes in the graph. While the virtual nodes were originally thought as representations of the entire graph, they also provide shortcuts for message passing between nodes along the graph edges. Surprisingly, the impact of virtual nodes for the link prediction problem has not been investigated yet. The reason for this might be that the often very large and heterogeneous “network” graphs in link prediction are of very different nature and require novel/adapted solutions (e.g., a protein interaction network may easily contain millions of nodes, whereas a molecule to be classified contains usually less than fifty).

Figure 1: We cluster graph nodes belonging together (gray shades) and connect them to a common virtual node (blue). This eases information exchange and hence prediction of links between nodes not directly related (red).
We explore application and effects of virtual nodes in link prediction theoretically and empirically:

- _We propose to use multiple virtual nodes in the link prediction scenario and describe a graph-based technique to connect them to the graph nodes._ Consider Figure 1. In a nutshell, we use a graph clustering algorithm to determine groups of nodes in the graph that belong together and then connect these nodes to a common virtual node. In this way, under-reaching is decreased because clustered nodes can share information easily; at the same time, the nodes are spared of unnecessary information from unrelated nodes (i.e., in contrast to the single virtual node model).
- We also investigate alternative methods to determine the virtual node connections (e.g., randomization in clustering) and compare to the original model with a single virtual node.
- We _theoretically investigate the benefit of using (multiple) virtual nodes_ in terms of two aspects: influence score and the expressiveness in learning a structural link representation.

- We conducted extensive experiments over challenging datasets of different type, provide ablation studies that confirm the superiority of our proposed techniques, analyze the results in detail, and _provide first guidelines about how to use virtual nodes with different types of data and GNNs_ .

- Most importantly, we show that _our virtual node extensions most often yield rather stable performance increases_ and allow standard GNNs to compete with complex state-of-the-art models that also try to improve message passing, as well as with the models leading the OGB leaderboards.

## 2 RELATED WORK

We give an overview on approaches that are similar from a technical perspective; for a more detailed summary, see Appendix A. For a more general overview of the large and diverse field of link prediction, we refer to good summaries in recent works (Martínez et al., 2016; Zhang et al., 2020).

**Deeper GNNs.** Several techniques address over-smoothing and hence allow for constructing deeper GNNs to solve under-reaching. These models range from the simple but efficient message propagation in SGC (Wu et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2020) and APPNP (Klicpera et al., 2019a) and connections in JKNet (Xu et al., 2018), to more advanced proposals (Chen et al., 2020; Sun et al., 2021; Zhou et al., 2020; Li et al., 2020a) such as the differentiable aggregation functions in DeeperGCN (Li et al., 2020a). However, although (Chen et al., 2020) show that over-smoothing happens particularly in dense graphs, the experiments in most of these works consider citation or recommendation networks, but not the especially dense and important biomedical ones. And our experiments over the latter suggest that the reported results are not generalizable to the more challenging biomedical data.

**Beyond One-Hop Neighbors.** Recently, graph diffusion methods are used in various ways to determine the message targets and thus extend standard message passing beyond the one-hop neighborhood. Atwood & Towsley (2016) use k-hop random walks to extend the node features. APPNP (Klicpera et al., 2019a) applies personalized PageRank to propagate the node predictions generated by a neural network. Other models concatenate (Abu-El-Haija et al., 2019) or aggregate (Xu et al., 2019a; Ma et al., 2020) node embeddings in every layer using a diffusion-based transition matrix. The diffusion-based graph neural network (GDC) (Klicpera et al., 2019b) aggregates information from multiple neighborhood hops at each layer by sparsifying a generalized form of graph diffusion. Subsequent works use diffusion methods on multiple scales (Liao et al., 2019; Luan et al., 2019; Xhonneux et al., 2020) and attention (Wang et al., 2020). Morris et al. (2019) take higher-order graph structures at multiple scales into account during message passing based on the _k_ -dimensional Weisfeiler and Leman graph algorithm. All the above approaches are relatively complex, many terminated with memory errors in our experiments, and few have been evaluated for link prediction.

**Virtual Nodes.** To the best of our knowledge, virtual nodes have only been considered in the context of graph classification so far, where a single virtual node (also called _supernode_ ) is added to the graph to be classified and connected to all graph nodes (Gilmer et al., 2017; Li et al., 2017; Pham et al., 2017; Ishiguro et al., 2019). Note that the original idea was to compute a graph embedding in parallel with the node embeddings and even connected the virtual node only in one direction (i.e, via edges from the graph nodes) instead of bidirectionally (Li et al., 2017).

There are some GNNs which point out special nodes that we could consider as “virtual”. Fey et al. (2020) propose a GNN for molecule graph classification which clusters certain nodes within a molecule using a structure-based, molecule-specific algorithm and then applies message passing within and between these clusters. The graph-partition based message passing from Liao et al. (2018) also used clustering, but it just divides the original messages into inter- and intra-cluster. Our approach creates new “paths” in the graph and we theoretically demonstrate its expressiveness. P-GNN (You et al., 2019) assigns nodes to random clusters (“anchor-sets”) and then creates a message for each node for every anchor-set, while ignoring the message passing from original direct neighbors. Our virtual nodes represent an alternative means to aggregate messages from multiple graph nodes which are not necessarily direct neighbors. We also explore the idea of similar random assignments in our context, but show that more elaborate techniques generally work better. Most importantly, we do not propose a specific, new GNN but a _new technique for augmenting existing graph neural networks_ .

Although it is a well-known trick, the advantage of using virtual nodes has never been theoretically investigated nor fully understood. We focus on link prediction and considerably extend the virtual node technique. There are commonalities in the advantages of using virtual nodes for graph classification and link prediction, but their role in link prediction is to improve the representation of the link instead of the graph (nodes). We analyze theoretically and empirically how they improve GNN performance.

## 3 PRELIMINARIES

**Link Prediction.** We consider an undirected _graph G_ = ( _V, E_ ) with _nodes V_ and _edges E ⊆ V × V_ . Note that this basic choice is only for ease of presentation. All our techniques work for directed graphs and, with simple adaptation, also for graphs with labelled edges. We assume _V_ to be ordered and may refer to a node by its index in _V_ . For a node _v ∈ V_ , _Nv_ denotes the set of its neighbors. Given two nodes, the _link prediction task_ is to predict whether there is a link between them.

**Message-Passing Graph Neural Networks.** In this paper, we usually use the term _graph neural networks_ (GNNs) to denote GNNs that use message passing as described by Gilmer et al. (2017). These networks compute for every _v ∈ V_ a node representation _h[ℓ] v_[at][layer] _[ℓ][∈]_[[1] _[,]_[ 2] _[, . . . , k]_[]][,][by] _aggregating_ its neighbor nodes based on a generic aggregation function and then _combine_ the obtained vector with _h[ℓ] v[−]_[1] as below; _h_[0] _v_[are the initial node features.]

Link prediction with GNNs is usually done by combining (e.g., concatenating) the final representations _h[L] u[, h][L] v[,]_[ of the nodes] _[ u, v]_[ under consideration and passing them through several feed-forward layers] with a final sigmoid function for scoring. We follow this approach.

We further use [1 _, n_ ] to denote an interval [1,2,..., n].

## 4 VIRTUAL NODES IN GRAPH NEURAL NETWORKS FOR LINK PREDICTION

So far, virtual nodes have been only used for graph classification. Link prediction scenarios are different in that the graphs are usually very large, heterogeneous, sometimes dense, and the task is to predict a relationship that might strongly be influenced depending on surrounding relations. In the following, we propose approaches that fit these scenarios.

## 4.1 MULTIPLE VIRTUAL NODES

Our main goal of using virtual nodes is to provide a shortcut for sharing information between the graph nodes. However, the amount of information in a graph with possibly millions of nodes is enormous, and likely too much to be captured in a single virtual node embedding. Further, not all information is equally relevant to all nodes. Therefore we suggest to use _multiple virtual nodes S_ = _{s_ 1 _, s_ 2 _. . . , sn}_[1] each being connected to a subset of graph nodes, as determined by an assignment _σ_ : _V →_ [1 _, n_ ]; _n_ is considered as hyperparameter. We propose different methods to obtain _σ_ :

**Random (GNN-RM).** Most simple, we can determine a fixed _σ_ randomly once with initialization.

**Increased Randomness (GNN-RM** _[F]_ **).** Similarly a random assignment, but initialized with every forward pass. In this way, a single bad assignment does not determine the overall performance.

**Clustering (GNN-CM).** Many types of graph data incorporate a certain cluster structure (e.g., collaboration or social networks) that reflects which nodes belong closely together. We propose to connect such nodes in a cluster to a common virtual node, such that the structure inherent to the given graph is reflected in our virtual node assignment _σ_ . More precisely, during initialization, we use a generic clustering algorithm which, given a number _m_ , creates a set _C_ = _{C_ 1 _, C_ 2 _. . . , Cm}_ of clusters (i.e., sets of graph nodes) by computing an assignment _ρ_ : _V →_ [1 _, m_ ], assigning each graph node to a cluster. We then obtain _σ_ by choosing _m_ = _n_ and _σ_ = _ρ_ .

In this work, we decided for the METIS clustering (Karypis & Kumar, 1998) which turned out to provide a good trade off between quality and efficiency. Nevertheless, our idea is generic and can be applied with arbitrary algorithms. We will show ablation experiments for alternatives (e.g., Graclus (Dhillon et al., 2007) and Diffpool (Ying et al., 2018b)).

**Advanced Clustering (GNN-CM**[+] **).** Not every type of graph data contains an inherent cluster structure or one that is sufficiently expressed. Furthermore, using a fixed clustering, we obtain a deterministic algorithm again taking the risk that we completely rely on a single, possibly not ideal, virtual node assignment – there may be critical long range dependencies that go beyond clusters. For these cases, we propose an alternative approach, which breaks up the determinism by extending the above clustering as follows. We choose a relatively large _m_ , with _m ≫ n_ , and apply the above clustering algorithm, during initialization. Then, in each epoch, we randomly guess an assignment _σ[′]_ : [1 _, m_ ] _→_ [1 _, n_ ] of clusters to virtual nodes and define _σ_ ( _n_ ) := _σ[′]_ ( _ρ_ ( _n_ )). Note that this approach is inspired by Chiang et al. (2019), who apply a similar technique is to create batches based on clusters. Further note that we determine _σ[′]_ with every epoch instead of every forward pass since the the computation takes quite some time on large datasets and we observed that this yields good results.

## 5 EVALUATION

We conducted extensive experiments and ablation studies to empirically investigate:

- How does the existing approach with **one virtual node perform in link prediction** ?

- **Do multiple virtual nodes improve performance** , how do our proposed approaches compare?

- In particular, **are approaches based on the graph structure better** ?

- How exactly do virtual nodes support link prediction? **When do they help particularly?**

**Datasets.** We focused on challenging data from the OGB: `ddi` , a drug-drug interaction network; `ppa10` , a subset of the protein-protein association network `ppa` containing only 10% of the train edges (but full valid/test); and `collab` , an author collaboration network. To learn more about smaller data of similar type, we also tested on the citation networks `pubmed` (Yang et al., 2016). Since the datasets are not only very different in type but also in various other critical graph parameters and this is reflected in the performance of the models, we show relevant statistics in Table 1.[3] The datasets vary strongly in size with `ddi` being smallest among the biomedical; on the other hand, `ddi` is very dense. The clustering coefficient intuitively reflects the “cliquishness” of the graph’s subgraphs. The large diameters suggest that the data suits testing under-reaching. Appendix C gives further details and describes datsets we consider in additional experiments in the appendix.
**Baselines** For a competitive comparison, we considered important baselines (described in Section 2):

- The deep GNNs **SGC** , **APPNP** , **DeeperGCN** , and two variants of **JKNet** .

- Approaches extending message passing: **P-GNN** , **APPNP** , **GCN-GDC** , **SAGE-GDC** , **GIN-GDC** .
- The popular GNNs **GCN** (Kipf & Welling, 2017), **SAGE** (Hamilton et al., 2017), and **GIN** (Xu et al., 2019b), which we then extend with (multiple) virtual nodes.

## 5.1 RESULTS

**Overall Impact of Virtual Nodes, Tables 2, 7, 8 (Appendix).** We compare to GCN, SAGE, and GIN. The common approach of using a single virtual node (GNN-VN) yields good improvements over `ddi` , slight improvements over `collab` , but no definitive ones over `ppa10` ; over `pubmed` , it works very well for GIN. The numbers for GNN-RM and GNN-RM _[F]_ reflect the randomness of their connections to the virtual nodes, there is no clear trend. Nevertheless, they clearly outperform the original models, with only few exceptions. The increased randomness by re-assigning the virtual nodes with every forward pass (GNN-RM _[F]_ ) seemingly suits SAGE but not the others. As expected, over the small `pubmed` / `cora` , which also have no cluster structure, the results are not consistent or convincing overall; virtual nodes only yield improvement sometimes, and none for GCN. Yet, on the more challenging datasets, _multiple virtual nodes turn out to be an efficient means to boost the link prediction performance of GNNs_ if they are applied correctly. Our virtual node connections based on the graph structure (GNN-CM) yield consistently good improvements over `ddi` and `collab` , and mostly help on the challenging `ppa10` dataset. On `collab` , we did further experiments using GAT (Veliˇckovi´c et al., 2017) and also observe a clear performance gain: 0.4745 vs. 0.5876 (GAT-CM). GNN-CM and GNN-CM[+] are not always the best ones, but yield reliably good results, in contrast to the other models with virtual nodes (see variability of gray shades). Interestingly, the advanced clustering yields especially good performance over `ppa10` / `ppa` , while its results on the other datasets are not convincing. Generally, the improvements of the virtual node models are strongest on `ddi` . For an in-depth result analysis see Section 5.2.

**Comparison to Related Works and SOTA, Table 3.** Most deep GNNs as well as the models that use complex message-passing techniques perform disappointing and, overall, much worse than the standard GNNs. We did thorough hyperparameter tuning for these models and it is hard to explain. However, most of the original evaluations focus on node or graph classification and consider very different types of data – often the standard citation networks (Lu & Getoor, 2003) – and, in fact, on `collab` we see the best numbers. For a more detailed discussion of P-GNN see Appendix H. Even if we assume that these numbers can be improved, the models do not seem apt for link prediction; in particular, the complex ones: many do not run at all on realistic link prediction data but yield memory errors. Further, _our virtual node extensions make standard GNNs competitive to the models on the leaderboard_ . In particular, their performance is much more stable. The results of the best models from the leaderboard vary strongly with the different datasets, or have not been reported at all. None of these models can be called “good” overall, given the numbers in the - sometimes even missing - rest of the table; in fact, SEAL and Adamic Adar perform rather bad on the very dense `ddi` .

**Impact of Virtual Nodes on Number of GNN Layers and Efficiency, Figure 2.** For the virtual nodes models, the scores increase with the number of layers for a longer time, GCN drops earlier. On `ddi` , GCN-VN and -CM reach their best scores at 6 and 8 layers, respectively, which is remarkable for that very dense dataset, which is prone to over-smoothing. On `collab` it is the other way around. The figure also gives an idea about the runtime increase with using virtual nodes. It compares the 6-layer models, and shows the 4-layer GCN-CM which obtains performance similar to the 6-layer GCN-VN.

**Impact of Virtual Node Number, Figure 3.** First, consider the configurations of the best models for the overall results in Table 2, which are provided in Table 6 in the appendix. Here, we see that the chosen numbers of virtual nodes are indeed random for the “random” models, but GNN-CM consistently uses a high number of virtual nodes, which also suits it better according to our theoretical analysis in Section 4.3. In line with this, the more detailed analysis varying the numbers of virtual nodes, yields best results (also in terms of standard deviations) for SAGE-CM at rather high values. For GCN, we do not see a clear trend, but (second) best performance with 64 virtual nodes. Note that there is a trade off between number of virtual nodes and intra-cluster test edges, discussed in Section 5.2.

**Using Virtual Nodes Only at the Last GNN Layer, Table 4.** Alon & Yahav (2021) show that using a fully connected adjacency matrix at the last layer of a standard GNN helps to better capture information over long ranges. We therefore investigated if it is a better architectural choice to use virtual nodes only at the last layer. However, we see that this can lead to extreme performance drops.

**Impact of Clustering Algorithm, Table 9 (Appendix).** Our architecture is generic in the clustering algorithm, and we investigated the effects of varying that. Graclus is similar in nature to METIS in that it also creates partitions based on the adjacency matrix, but it took much longer to run. Diffpool considers the node features and yields improvements for GCN, but does not scale to larger datasets. Over `ddi` , there is no clear winner and, given its efficiency, METIS turns out to be a good solution.

## 5.2 DISCUSSION AND CONCLUSIONS

The results show that our approach with multiple virtual nodes based on graph-based clustering yields performance increases for various GNNs and types of data, but there are clear differences.

**Dense Graphs with Medium/High Clustering Coefficient.** Over `ddi` , we see strongest improvements for all virtual-node models. This can be explained by our proposed theory, showing that a very large node degree _m_ increases the impact of the virtual node(s), and thus decreases the negative impact of the (too) many other neighbors (see Equation (6)). Furthermore, the empirical results confirm our proposed theory regarding multiple virtual nodes (see Equation (7)). We see particularly good numbers for GNN-CM, which exploits the clustering inherent in the given graph. GNN-CM[+] , which considers this given clustering only on a lower level, is shown to perform worse than GNN-CM overall. In fact, we computed the percentage of test edges that occur in the “virtual node cluster” (see Table 11 in the appendix) and it shows that the numbers for the advanced clustering are very similar to the random one, meaning the randomly merged smaller clusters break the data’s structure too much. Interestingly, the experiments show that, even with the dense data that is prone to over-smoothing, virtual nodes make the GNNs score higher with more than the standard 2-3 layers; hence virtual nodes seem to alleviate over-smoothing to some extent, an interesting question for future work.

> **Graphs with Large Problem Radius and Low Clustering Coefficient.** Over `ppa10` , all GNNs use an unusually high number of layers, which hints at a large problem radius (e.g., GCN, which performs especially well, uses 7 layers). Given the very low clustering of the data in addition, `ppa10` represents a special challenge. With the multiple virtual nodes, GNN-CM performs again better than GNN-VN. On the other hand, it does not perform much better than the random models on data without cluster structure. This can be explained by its choice of number of virtual nodes, which is consistently high, but then there are less test edges within a virtual node cluster (see appendix Table 11). We hence see here that the positive effect of having many virtual nodes (recall Equation (7)) cancel out the benefits of clustering. Our advanced clustering, which merges some local clustering with randomness, is able to achieve best results with GCN and GIN (with SAGE, all models perform rather bad over `ppa10` ). This can be explained by the fact that it randomly merges some local clusters – with each epoch anew – and hence allows more messages to pass across “virtual node clusters”. We also did some experiments over the very large `ppa` , which is denser than `ppa10` , and see a similar trend.

**Sparse Graphs with Low to High Clustering Coefficient.** We tested on three citation/collaboration networks of different sizes. Note that, over this data, the problem radius is usually assumed to be rather small (Alon & Yahav, 2021), although the graph diameters are large. We investigated virtual nodes to augment link prediction in large and complex graphs; but we also want to provide insight into the behavior on smaller data. Over `pubmed` (similarly on `cora` as shown in the appendix), virtual nodes do not provide any improvement for GCN. For GIN, a single virtual node yields good increases; overall, it usually outperforms the settings with multiple virtual nodes. We hypothesize that this is mainly due to the small graph size and sparsity. In fact, on the larger and denser `collab` , GNN-CM performs very good for all GNNs. The trends in the models’ performance and the corresponding explanations are similar to those for `ddi` but much less expressed, probably due to the much smaller node degrees. Yet, the performance is much more stable, possibly because it is larger and not as dense.
**Conclusions.** We summarize our main findings to provide first guidelines for applying virtual nodes:
- **Small + Sparse Graphs:** A single virtual node is likely to boost performance of GIN, and virtual nodes should help with SAGE, but probably not with GCN.

- **Large + Sparse Graphs** : If there is cluster structure, GNN-CM should yield stable performance increases. If the problem radius is large or there is few cluster structure, GNN-CM[+] is worth a try.

- **Dense Graphs + Clustering:** Multiple virtual nodes (i.e., GNN-CM) likely increase performance.

## 6 CONCLUSIONS

We propose a simple but elegant graph neural network extension using multiple virtual nodes that may considerably increase link prediction performance. We also advance research by providing theoretical justifications - the very first about applying virtual nodes at all - and by showing their positive impact in various experiments. Future work includes the design of more advanced and scalable architectures, and it would be interesting to further investigate the huge performance increases on dense graphs.

coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Coarse (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback:
This paper investigates the use of multiple virtual nodes as a mechanism to mitigate under-reaching in GNNs for link prediction. By employing graph clustering to determine virtual node connectivity, the authors aim to improve message passing in large, dense, and heterogeneous graphs, providing an empirical evaluation on OGB datasets.

The paper addresses a practical and relevant challenge in GNN-based link prediction by proposing a flexible virtual node architecture. While the empirical results on OGB datasets show promise, the theoretical justification for why multiple virtual nodes specifically address the identified limitations remains somewhat loose. The work is a solid contribution that would benefit from more rigorous analysis of the trade-offs between computational overhead and performance gains.

Overview issues:

- Unclear Theoretical Link between Multiple Virtual Nodes and Expressivity: In Section 4.3, the authors argue that multiple virtual nodes improve expressiveness, but the connection to the labeling trick and distance encoding is only briefly mentioned in the appendix. The theoretical derivation in Equation (7) assumes a simplified random walk model that may not capture the complex relational dynamics of actual link prediction. To strengthen the paper, the authors should provide a more formal characterization of what structural features are captured by the virtual nodes. Specifically, they should clarify if these nodes act as global aggregators or if they effectively learn a form of structural embedding that substitutes for explicit distance features.

- Inconsistent Performance Gains on Sparse Datasets: The empirical results in Table 2 show that the proposed methods (GNN-CM, GNN-CM+) do not consistently outperform baselines on sparse datasets like pubmed. The authors hypothesize that this is due to the small graph size and sparsity, but this limitation is not fully integrated into the proposed guidelines. It is unclear if the virtual node approach is truly generalizable or if it is strictly optimized for dense, high-clustering-coefficient networks. A more thorough analysis of why performance drops or remains stagnant in sparse regimes would significantly improve the paper's contribution.

- Computational Overhead and Scalability Discussion: While the paper mentions memory errors for other complex models, it lacks a detailed analysis of the computational overhead introduced by the proposed multiple virtual node approach. The runtime comparison in Figure 2 is a good start, but it does not account for the additional cost of the clustering algorithm (METIS) during preprocessing or the potential for increased training time as the number of virtual nodes grows. For a method targeting large-scale link prediction, providing a clear Big-O complexity analysis regarding the number of virtual nodes and graph density is essential for practitioners.

- Sensitivity to Clustering Quality: The methodology relies heavily on the quality of the initial clustering (e.g., METIS). However, the paper does not systematically explore how the choice of clustering algorithm or the number of clusters affects the final link prediction performance beyond a brief mention in the appendix. If the performance is highly sensitive to the clustering structure, the proposed method might be less robust than claimed. The authors should include an ablation study that compares different clustering granularities and their impact on the stability of the model across various graph types.

- Lack of empirical validation for the influence score hypothesis: The authors derive an influence score in Section 4.3 to justify the benefit of multiple virtual nodes, claiming that the impact grows with the number of nodes and the graph's degree. However, this theoretical result is not verified with a controlled simulation. To demonstrate that the result has bite, the authors should compute the empirical influence score on a synthetic graph (e.g., a Stochastic Block Model) and compare it against the theoretical value derived in Equation (7). This would confirm whether the theoretical model accurately captures the behavior of the GNNs in practice or if it relies on overly restrictive assumptions about the message-passing dynamics.

- Missing implementation guidance for the clustering hyperparameter: The paper relies on METIS clustering to define virtual node connectivity, yet it provides no systematic guidance on how to select the number of clusters (m) or virtual nodes (n) for a new dataset. As these choices appear to significantly affect performance, this gap leaves practitioners without a clear path to deployment. The authors should provide a heuristic or a sensitivity analysis plot showing performance as a function of the ratio of clusters to total nodes, specifically for graphs with varying density, to help users calibrate the model for their own applications.

- Absence of a formal comparison to the 'Labeling Trick' baseline: The authors claim that their multiple virtual node approach is related to the labeling trick (e.g., SEAL) and distance encoding, yet they do not include these as direct baselines in their primary performance tables. While SEAL is mentioned in Table 3, it is not used to evaluate the same GNN architectures (GCN, SAGE, GIN) to isolate the benefit of the virtual node mechanism versus the structural information provided by labeling. A direct comparison where the same base GNN is augmented with either virtual nodes or standard distance-based labeling would clarify whether the proposed method provides a distinct advantage or simply acts as a proxy for existing structural encoding techniques.

Recommendation: major revision. The paper provides an interesting empirical direction, but the theoretical claims regarding the expressiveness of multiple virtual nodes require more rigorous grounding. Additionally, the inconsistency in performance on sparse datasets needs a more comprehensive explanation to guide future applications.

Revision targets:

- Provide a more formal theoretical analysis connecting multiple virtual nodes to structural feature learning, moving beyond the simple random walk influence score.

- Include a detailed computational complexity analysis regarding the number of virtual nodes and the overhead of the clustering process.

- Expand the ablation study on clustering strategies to demonstrate the robustness of the method across different graph topologies.

- Refine the guidelines for practitioners by explicitly addressing the failure cases observed on sparse datasets.

comments.title: Inconsistency in SAGE collab performance reporting
comments.explanation: The performance reported for SAGE on the 'collab' dataset in Table 3 (0.5516 ± 0.0171) contradicts the value reported for the same model in Table 2 (0.5662 ± 0.0149). These values should be identical as they refer to the same baseline model on the same dataset. Please verify the source of these discrepancies and ensure consistency across all tables.

comments.title: Inaccurate claim regarding virtual node investigation
comments.quote: Surprisingly, the impact of virtual nodes for the link prediction problem has not been investigated yet.
comments.explanation: This claim is overbroad. Virtual nodes have been utilized in link prediction contexts prior to this work, including in knowledge graph completion and studies exploring global message passing. Please refine this claim to specify that the systematic study of multiple virtual nodes or their specific application to the OGB-style link prediction task is the novel contribution.

comments.title: Algebraic error in impact ratio approximation
comments.quote: When $m$ is large enough, $ir$ can be approximated by $ir\simeq\left(1+\frac{m^{k-1}}{|V||R^{k}|}\right)$.
comments.explanation: The approximation $ir \simeq 1 + \frac{m^{k-1}}{|V||R^k|}$ is mathematically inconsistent with the provided expression $ir = \frac{m^k}{(m+1)^k} + \frac{m^k}{(m+1)|V||R^k|}$. Using the Taylor expansion $(1+1/m)^{-k} \approx 1 - k/m$, the first term is $1 - k/m + O(m^{-2})$. The authors' approximation incorrectly drops the $-k/m$ term, which is significant when $m$ is large. Please remove the approximation or include the $O(1/m)$ correction term.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim of theoretical justification
comments.quote: We also advance research by providing theoretical justifications - the very first about applying virtual nodes at all - and by showing their positive impact in various experiments.
comments.explanation: The paper claims to provide theoretical justifications, yet the formal content is limited to definitions and heuristic derivations in Section 4.3. It does not contain formal theorems, lemmas, or proofs regarding the properties or performance of virtual nodes. Please either include the formal proofs that support these justifications or remove the claim of providing theoretical justifications.

comments.title: Ambiguity in the scope of graph size and density
comments.quote: In this paper, we adapt the concept of virtual nodes to the link prediction scenario, where we usually have much larger, often dense, and more heterogeneous graphs.
comments.explanation: The claim that link prediction graphs are 'often dense' is misleading. Most real-world link prediction datasets, including those in the OGB benchmark, are characterized by high sparsity. Describing these graphs as 'often dense' contradicts the empirical reality of the field. Please clarify whether the method is specifically designed for dense subgraphs or if the claim refers to the connectivity density of the virtual nodes themselves.

comments.title: Variable collision in functional notation
comments.quote: and define $\sigma(n):=\sigma^{\prime}(\rho(n))$.
comments.explanation: The variable $n$ is defined earlier in the text as the total number of virtual nodes. Using $n$ as the input argument for the function $\sigma$ is a variable collision that makes the expression ambiguous. The intended argument is a graph node $v \in V$. Please replace '$\sigma(n):=\sigma^{\prime}(\rho(n))$' with '$\sigma(v):=\sigma^{\prime}(\rho(v))$' for all $v \in V$ to maintain consistency.

coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Coarse (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback:
The paper explores adding virtual nodes to standard GNNs to improve link prediction on large, heterogeneous graphs. The authors propose using multiple virtual nodes connected via graph clustering and provide theoretical and empirical evaluations on OGB datasets. While the idea of augmenting message passing with auxiliary nodes is intuitive, the execution and analysis contain several methodological gaps that need addressing before publication.

This work tackles a practical problem in link prediction by introducing a simple architectural modification that can be plugged into existing GNNs. The empirical evaluation across diverse datasets demonstrates that the approach can yield consistent gains, particularly on dense graphs. The paper is well-organized and addresses a relevant gap in the literature regarding virtual node applications beyond graph classification.

Overview issues:

- Information Leakage via Global Graph Clustering: The clustering step described in Section 4.1 uses the full graph structure to assign nodes to virtual nodes. The authors state they apply METIS during initialization but do not clarify whether this operates on the complete adjacency matrix or only the training edges. In link prediction, constructing clusters from the full graph inevitably leaks information about test edges, artificially inflating performance. This violates standard inductive or transductive evaluation protocols used in OGB benchmarks. The revision must explicitly restrict the clustering algorithm to the training subgraph or adopt a fully inductive strategy that does not depend on held-out edges.

- Flawed Theoretical Derivation for Influence Scores: Section 4.3 attempts to justify virtual nodes through random walk probabilities, but the mathematical derivation rests on unrealistic assumptions. Equation (5) assumes that a message routed through a virtual node has a uniform transition probability of $1/((m+1)|V|)$, which ignores the actual feature aggregation mechanism and the learnable parameters in Equation (2). The model does not perform a uniform random walk; it aggregates node embeddings and passes them through linear layers and normalization. Consequently, the derived impact ratio in Equation (6) does not accurately reflect how virtual nodes alter message passing dynamics. The authors should replace this heuristic probability argument with a more rigorous analysis of the GNN effective receptive field or provide empirical measurements of actual influence propagation.

- Unreported Computational Overhead and Scalability: Adding virtual nodes that connect to subsets of nodes significantly increases the number of edges processed during message passing. The paper claims the method is efficient and scalable, yet it completely omits computational metrics like training time, memory footprint, or FLOPs. Table 2 shows that adding virtual nodes sometimes increases variance without proportional gains, which makes the computational cost a critical factor for practitioners. Without reporting these overheads, readers cannot assess whether the performance gains justify the architectural changes. The authors need to include a detailed efficiency analysis comparing baseline GNNs against their augmented versions across all datasets.

- High Result Variance and Missing Statistical Tests: Table 2 reports standard deviations that frequently exceed 0.10, and occasionally reach 0.17 for configurations like GCN-CM on the ddi dataset. Such high variance indicates that the proposed methods are sensitive to random initialization and hyperparameter choices. The abstract and introduction claim stable performance increases, which directly contradicts the empirical results presented in the tables. The paper lacks statistical significance testing to determine whether the observed improvements are reliable or merely noise. The authors should report confidence intervals, run additional seeds, and apply statistical tests to substantiate their claims of stability.

- Inconsistent Baseline Reporting and SOTA Comparison: Table 3 compares the proposed method against OGB leaderboard results and several custom baselines, but the evaluation setup lacks transparency. Several complex message-passing models like P-GNN and GDC variants yield unexpectedly low performance on the ddi dataset, suggesting potential issues with hyperparameter tuning or implementation details. Furthermore, the paper introduces adapted SAGE models without explaining how these modifications differ from standard implementations. A fair comparison requires reproducing these baselines under identical splitting, negative sampling, and optimization protocols. The authors must clarify the baseline configurations and provide a more controlled ablation against recent link prediction methods.

- Worked example grounding the influence score derivation: Readers cannot calibrate the intuition behind Equations (5) and (7) without a concrete, fully-specified model. The theoretical analysis relies on asymptotic arguments for regular graphs and heuristic random walk probabilities. Compute the exact influence score and message-passing dynamics for a small parametric graph, such as a 6-node star or a 4-node path with a single virtual node. Show step-by-step how the virtual node alters the receptive field compared to the baseline. This demonstration would prove the result has bite and clarify the exact conditions under which the theoretical impact ratio holds.

- Controlled simulation isolating long-range dependency effects: A natural question is whether the method actually captures long-range dependencies as claimed. The empirical evaluation only reports results on large, heterogeneous OGB datasets where many confounding factors exist. Add a simulation on a synthetic graph with a known data generating process that contains planted long-range edges or specific community structures. Measure how the virtual node configuration affects prediction accuracy on these specific edges versus local edges. This would verify that the performance gains stem from the proposed mechanism rather than general capacity increases.

- Missing inference protocol and clustering implementation details: It remains unclear how the virtual node assignments are handled during inference, whether the assignment is fixed after training, or how the method scales to graphs where the full adjacency matrix is not available. The authors state that clustering is applied during initialization but omit critical implementation details needed for reproducibility and practical deployment. Readers will wonder how to apply this technique to new graphs or dynamic settings. The authors should provide a clear algorithmic description of the clustering step, specify how many virtual nodes are selected relative to graph size, and clarify the inference procedure.

- Missing verification of expressiveness claims against baselines: The expressiveness claims in the abstract and Section 4.3 lack formal verification or comparison against the mentioned baselines. The paper draws parallels to the labeling trick and distance encoding but never demonstrates that the proposed architecture actually surpasses these methods in representational capacity. A straightforward check would be to test whether the virtual node approach distinguishes graph structures that standard GNNs or distance-encoded models cannot. The authors should include a theoretical or empirical comparison showing how the architecture exceeds the 1-WL test in specific graph families to substantiate the claim.

Recommendation: Major revision

Revision targets:

- Restrict the graph clustering algorithm to the training subgraph or implement a fully inductive clustering strategy to prevent test edge leakage.

- Replace the random walk probability derivation in Section 4.3 with a rigorous analysis of the virtual node aggregation mechanism or provide empirical influence measurements.

- Report training time, memory usage, and FLOPs for all model configurations to justify the computational cost of the proposed method.

- Provide statistical significance tests and additional random seeds to address the high variance observed in Table 2 and substantiate claims of stability.

- Clarify baseline implementations, reproduce complex SOTA models under identical protocols, and explain the modifications made to the adapted SAGE variants.

comments.title: Contradiction between stability claims and empirical variance
comments.quote:
in detail. We show that our virtual node extensions yield rather stable performance increases and allow standard graph neural networks to compete with complex state-of-the-art models, as well as with the models leading the OGB leaderboards.

## 1 Introduction
comments.explanation: The assertion of stable performance increases clashes directly with Table 2. Standard deviations frequently exceed 0.10 and reach 0.17 for configurations like GCN-CM on the ddi dataset. Such high variance points to strong sensitivity to random initialization rather than stability. The introduction sets an expectation that the data does not support. Temper this claim in the abstract and introduction, or run additional seeds and apply statistical significance tests to substantiate the reliability of the improvements.

comments.title: Claim of extreme drops contradicts Table 4 data
comments.quote: However, we see that this can lead to extreme performance drops.
comments.explanation: Table 4 shows mixed outcomes when virtual nodes are applied only at the last layer. GCN actually improves (0.6180 vs 0.5932), and GIN remains nearly identical (0.6472 vs 0.6523). Only SAGE shows a significant drop. The statement overgeneralizes from the SAGE case. Adjust the description to reflect that the impact is model-dependent rather than universally detrimental.

comments.title: Overstated theoretical novelty and lack of formal support
comments.quote: We also advance research by providing theoretical justifications - the very first about applying virtual nodes at all - and by showing their positive impact in various experiments.
comments.explanation: This assertion overstates the paper's contribution. The analysis in Section 4.3 relies on heuristic random walk probabilities rather than a rigorous derivation of message-passing dynamics. The conclusion promises formal theoretical justifications, but the paper only presents heuristic equations without theorems or proofs. Soften the claim to reflect a novel heuristic analysis, or add the promised formal results. Readers expect a structured theoretical contribution when this language is used, and the current draft falls short of that standard.

comments.title: Contradiction between conclusion and empirical results
comments.quote: it would be interesting to further investigate the huge performance increases on dense graphs.
comments.explanation: Describing the outcomes as huge performance increases ignores the high variance documented in Table 2. The conclusion should accurately reflect the moderate gains with high sensitivity to hyperparameters. Alternatively, the authors should explain the variance before claiming huge improvements, as the current wording misleads readers about the method's reliability.

comments.title: Mischaracterization of diffusion methods for link prediction
comments.quote: All the above approaches are relatively complex, many terminated with memory errors in our experiments, and few have been evaluated for link prediction.
comments.explanation: Diffusion-based methods like APPNP and GDC are widely adopted and extensively evaluated for link prediction. Stating that few have been evaluated misrepresents the literature. Acknowledge their established use and clarify how the proposed augmentation specifically differs or improves upon them in this setting.

comments.title: Overstated novelty regarding virtual node applications
comments.quote: es. To the best of our knowledge, virtual nodes have only been considered in the context of graph classification so far, where a single virtual node (also called *supernode*) is added to the graph to be classified and connected to all graph nodes *(Gilmer et al., 2017; Li et al., 2017; Pham et al., 2017; Ishiguro et al., 2019)*.
comments.explanation: Virtual nodes have been explored in node classification, link prediction, and zero-shot learning to mitigate oversquashing. Claiming exclusive use in graph classification overlooks relevant prior art. Revise the related work to accurately position the technique across multiple paradigms and highlight the specific contributions to link prediction.

comments.title: Inaccurate description of P-GNN methodology
comments.quote: -GNN *(You et al., 2019)* assigns nodes to random clusters (“anchor-sets”) and then creates a message for each node for every anchor-set, while ignoring the message passing from original direct neighbors.
comments.explanation: P-GNN optimizes a sparse, task-specific adjacency matrix for structure learning. It does not rely on random anchor-sets or discard direct neighbor messages. The characterization appears to conflate P-GNN with anchor-based architectures. Correct the description to match the cited work to ensure a fair comparison.

comments.title: Conceptual mismatch in clustering alternatives
comments.quote: ill show ablation experiments for alternatives (e.g., Graclus *(Dhillon et al., 2007)* and Diffpool *(Ying et al., 2018b)*).
comments.explanation: Diffpool learns soft assignments dynamically rather than partitioning the graph into fixed clusters like METIS. Using it as a direct alternative for the fixed clustering step is conceptually mismatched. Clarify the testing protocol or replace it with a proper fixed clustering algorithm to maintain consistency with the described method.

comments.title: Factual inaccuracy regarding OGB link prediction dataset density

comments.explanation: Standard OGB link prediction benchmarks are characteristically sparse. Describing them as dense misrepresents the evaluation setting and contradicts the motivation for using clustering to sparsify connections. Adjust the characterization to match the actual dataset properties, or remove the density claim entirely.

coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Coarse (deepseek-v4-flash)
coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback:
The paper adapts virtual nodes—a technique from graph classification—to link prediction, proposing multiple virtual nodes with clustering-based connections. It provides some theoretical analysis and extensive experiments on OGB datasets. The main strength is the simplicity of the approach and the empirical gains on dense graphs like ddi. However, the paper has several significant issues regarding theoretical rigor, experimental design, and clarity of claims.

Overview issues:

- Theoretical analysis of influence scores is flawed and incomplete: The influence score analysis in Section 4.3 contains several problems. Equation (5) adds probabilities from two independent walks (x→y and x→s→y) as if they are disjoint events, but they are not—the random walk can take multiple paths, and the probability of going through the virtual node is not simply 1/(m+1)|V| because the virtual node connects to all nodes, not just one. The derivation assumes a regular graph with degree m, but then uses average degree for general graphs without justification. The impact ratio in Equation (6) is derived incorrectly: the term |R^k| in the denominator is path-dependent and not a simple scalar. The claim that 'impact grows with m' is based on an approximation that ignores the |R^k| term, which itself depends on m. The analysis does not account for the fact that virtual nodes add edges, changing the graph structure and thus the random walk probabilities in a more complex way. The paper needs a rigorous derivation or should clearly state the simplifying assumptions and limitations.

- No clear distinction between inductive and transductive settings: The paper claims to handle both inductive and transductive link prediction but never explicitly defines which setting is used in the experiments. The OGB datasets have specific train/validation/test splits that are transductive (same graph, different edges), but the virtual node assignment (especially clustering) is done on the full graph during initialization. This means the virtual node connections are informed by the test edges, which is a form of data leakage. For inductive settings (predicting links in unseen graphs), the clustering would need to be recomputed, but the paper does not discuss how to handle this. The paper should clearly state the setting for each experiment, discuss whether clustering uses test information, and provide results for a purely inductive scenario if claimed.

- Baseline comparisons are unfair and incomplete: The paper compares against state-of-the-art models from the OGB leaderboard (Table 3) but uses different evaluation settings. The leaderboard models often use additional features (e.g., SAGE+attr, SAGE+dist) or different training procedures, while the virtual node models use only basic node features. The paper claims that virtual nodes 'allow standard GNNs to compete with complex state-of-the-art models,' but the comparison is not apples-to-apples. For example, SEAL uses subgraph extraction and labeling, which is fundamentally different from simple GNNs. The paper should either (a) re-implement leaderboard models with the same features and training setup, or (b) clearly state that the comparison is against reported numbers with different settings. Additionally, the paper does not compare against other simple techniques to improve message passing, such as adding residual connections or using attention, which would isolate the benefit of virtual nodes.

- No statistical significance testing or multiple run analysis: The results in Tables 2 and 3 report means and standard deviations over multiple runs, but there is no statistical significance testing (e.g., paired t-tests, confidence intervals) to determine whether improvements are meaningful. Given the high variance in many results (e.g., GCN on ddi has standard deviations of 0.10-0.18), many of the claimed improvements may not be statistically significant. The paper should include pairwise significance tests between the baseline GNN and its virtual node variants, or at least report effect sizes and confidence intervals. Without this, the claim that virtual nodes 'yield rather stable performance increases' is not supported by the data.

- The number of virtual nodes is treated as a free hyperparameter without guidance: The paper acknowledges that the number of virtual nodes n is a hyperparameter but provides no principled way to choose it. The ablation study in Figure 3 shows that performance varies significantly with n, and the optimal value differs across datasets and GNN architectures. The paper's guidelines in the conclusion do not mention how to select n. For practitioners, this is a critical omission—the method's success depends on tuning n, which requires extensive validation. The paper should either provide a heuristic for choosing n (e.g., based on graph size or clustering coefficient) or demonstrate that the method is robust to a range of values.

- Clustering algorithm choice and its impact on generalization are underexplored: The paper uses METIS clustering as the default, with brief ablation on Graclus and Diffpool (Table 9 in appendix). However, METIS is a graph partitioning algorithm that minimizes edge cuts, which may not align with the goal of grouping nodes that need to share information for link prediction. The paper does not discuss whether the clustering should be based on graph structure, node features, or both. More importantly, the clustering is done once on the full graph (including test nodes), which may not generalize to new nodes in inductive settings. The paper should discuss the inductive case and provide experiments where clustering is done only on training nodes or using a scalable online clustering method.

- The claim that virtual nodes alleviate over-smoothing is not substantiated: In Section 5.2, the paper states that 'virtual nodes seem to alleviate over-smoothing to some extent' based on the observation that performance peaks at higher layer counts for virtual node models (Figure 2). However, over-smoothing is typically measured by the similarity of node representations (e.g., cosine similarity between nodes), not by performance curves. The paper does not provide any direct evidence (e.g., representation similarity analysis, Dirichlet energy) that virtual nodes reduce over-smoothing. The performance increase with more layers could be due to other factors, such as increased model capacity or better gradient flow. The paper should either provide direct over-smoothing metrics or temper this claim.

- No worked example or simulation for theoretical claims: The paper's theoretical analysis in Section 4.3 derives influence scores and expressiveness benefits for virtual nodes, but it never computes these quantities for a concrete, fully-specified model. For example, the paper could compute the influence score ratio in Equation (6) for a small regular graph (e.g., a cycle or grid) with and without virtual nodes, showing numerically that the ratio exceeds 1. This would let readers calibrate intuitions about when virtual nodes help. Without such a worked example, the theoretical claims remain abstract and unverified. The paper should add a simple simulation on a small synthetic graph (e.g., a 10-node cycle) where it computes the exact random walk probabilities and influence scores, confirming the predicted improvement.

- Missing discussion of estimation and implementation for inductive setting: The paper claims to handle both inductive and transductive link prediction but never explains how the virtual node assignment (clustering) is computed in an inductive setting where the test graph is unseen. The clustering is done once on the full graph during initialization, which leaks test information. For a purely inductive scenario, the clustering would need to be recomputed on the training graph only, or a different assignment strategy would be needed. The paper should explicitly state the setting for each experiment, discuss whether clustering uses test nodes, and provide results for a clean inductive split (e.g., training on one graph, testing on a disjoint graph from the same distribution). Without this, the practical applicability of the method is unclear.

- No comparison to simple baselines that isolate virtual node benefit: The paper compares virtual node GNNs against complex state-of-the-art models and standard GNNs, but it does not compare against simple architectural changes that could achieve similar benefits, such as adding residual connections, skip connections, or attention mechanisms. For example, the paper could compare GCN with virtual nodes against GCN with residual connections or GCN with an additional fully connected layer. Without these ablations, it is unclear whether the performance gains come from the virtual nodes themselves or simply from increased model capacity. The paper should include experiments comparing virtual node GNNs to GNNs with additional parameters (e.g., extra linear layers) or with residual/skip connections, using the same total parameter count.

- No guidance on choosing the number of virtual nodes: The paper treats the number of virtual nodes n as a free hyperparameter and shows in Figure 3 that performance varies significantly with n, yet it provides no heuristic or principled method for selecting n. The guidelines in the conclusion do not mention how to choose n. For practitioners, this omission makes the method difficult to apply without extensive validation. The paper should either derive a heuristic based on graph properties (e.g., number of nodes, clustering coefficient, average degree) or demonstrate that performance is robust across a wide range of n for each dataset. A simple suggestion would be to set n proportional to the square root of the number of nodes or to the number of clusters found by a standard clustering algorithm.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Fix the theoretical analysis of influence scores (Section 4.3) to correctly derive the impact of virtual nodes, clearly stating assumptions and limitations.

- Explicitly state the inductive vs. transductive setting for each experiment, and ensure that clustering does not use test information in transductive experiments. Add inductive link prediction experiments if claimed.

- Add statistical significance testing (e.g., paired t-tests) for all main results, and report confidence intervals or effect sizes.

- Provide guidance on choosing the number of virtual nodes, either through a heuristic or by showing robustness across a range of values.

- Include a direct analysis of over-smoothing (e.g., representation similarity, Dirichlet energy) to support the claim that virtual nodes alleviate it.

comments.title: Unfair baseline comparison with SOTA models
comments.quote: Most importantly, we show that our virtual node extensions most often yield rather stable performance increases and allow standard GNNs to compete with complex state-of-the-art models that also try to improve message passing, as well as with the models leading the OGB leaderboards.
comments.explanation: This claim is presented as a key contribution but is not supported by the evidence. The comparison in Table 3 is not apples-to-apples because leaderboard models (marked with *) use additional features (e.g., SAGE+attr, SAGE+dist) or different training procedures, while the virtual node models use only basic node features. The paper should either re-implement leaderboard models with the same features and training setup, or clearly state that the comparison is against reported numbers with different settings and temper the competitiveness claim.

comments.title: Missing inductive setting discussion and data leakage
comments.quote:
7 ± 0.0262  |

Datasets. We focused on challenging data from the OGB: ddi, a drug-drug interaction network; ppa10, a subset of the protein-protein association network ppa containing only  $10\%$  of the train edges (but full valid/test); and collab, an author collaboration network. To le
comments.explanation: The paper claims to handle both inductive and transductive link prediction but never explicitly defines which setting is used in the experiments. The OGB datasets have specific train/validation/test splits that are transductive (same graph, different edges), but the virtual node assignment (especially clustering) is done on the full graph during initialization. This means the virtual node connections are informed by the test edges, which is a form of data leakage. For inductive settings (predicting links in unseen graphs), the clustering would need to be recomputed, but the paper does not discuss how to handle this. The paper should clearly state the setting for each experiment, discuss whether clustering uses test information, and provide results for a purely inductive scenario if claimed.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim about over-smoothing alleviation
comments.quote: Interestingly, the experiments show that, even with the dense data that is prone to over-smoothing, virtual nodes make the GNNs score higher with more than the standard 2-3 layers; hence virtual nodes seem to alleviate over-smoothing to some extent, an interesting question for future work.
comments.explanation: The claim that virtual nodes 'seem to alleviate over-smoothing' is based solely on the observation that performance peaks at higher layer counts. Over-smoothing is a phenomenon where node representations become indistinguishable, which is measured by metrics like Dirichlet energy or cosine similarity between node embeddings, not by test accuracy. The paper provides no direct evidence (e.g., representation similarity analysis, Dirichlet energy plots) that virtual nodes reduce over-smoothing. The performance increase with more layers could be due to increased model capacity, better gradient flow, or the virtual nodes acting as a form of residual connection. The paper should either provide direct over-smoothing metrics or temper this claim to 'virtual nodes allow deeper GNNs without performance degradation' and remove the over-smoothing attribution.

comments.title: Missing statistical significance testing
comments.quote: Table 2: GCN, SAGE, and GIN with different virtual node configurations over different graph-data types/amounts; (second) best results are (light) gray, overall best bold, second best underlined.
comments.explanation: The table reports means and standard deviations, but there is no statistical significance testing (e.g., paired t-tests, confidence intervals) to determine whether the improvements of virtual node variants over the base GNN are meaningful. For example, GCN on ddi has a standard deviation of 0.1073, and the improvement from GCN (0.4076) to GCN-VN (0.6217) is large, but many other comparisons (e.g., GCN on ppa10: 0.1313 vs. GCN-CM: 0.1299) show overlapping error bars. Without significance tests, the claim that virtual nodes 'yield rather stable performance increases' is not supported by the data. Add pairwise significance tests between each baseline GNN and its virtual node variants, or at least report effect sizes and confidence intervals.

comments.title: Missing comparison to simple architectural baselines
comments.quote:
Baselines For a competitive comparison, we considered important baselines (described in Section 2):

- The deep GNNs SGC, APPNP, DeeperGCN, and two variants of JKNet.
- Approaches extending message passing: P-GNN, APPNP, GCN-GDC, SAGE-GDC, GIN-GDC.
- The popular GNNs GCN (Kipf & Welling, 2017), SAGE (Hamilton et al., 2017), and GIN (Xu et al., 2019b), which we then extend with (multiple) virtual nodes.
comments.explanation: The paper compares virtual node GNNs against complex state-of-the-art models and standard GNNs, but it does not compare against simple architectural changes that could achieve similar benefits, such as adding residual connections, skip connections, or attention mechanisms. For example, the paper could compare GCN with virtual nodes against GCN with residual connections or GCN with an additional fully connected layer. Without these ablations, it is unclear whether the performance gains come from the virtual nodes themselves or simply from increased model capacity. Add experiments comparing virtual node GNNs to GNNs with additional parameters (e.g., extra linear layers) or with residual/skip connections, using the same total parameter count.

comments.title: Contradictory claims about GNN-CM+ performance on ppa10
comments.quote: Interestingly, the advanced clustering yields especially good performance over ppa10/ppa, while its results on the other datasets are not convincing. Generally, the improvements of the virtual node models are strongest on ddi. For an in-depth result analysis see Section 5.2.
comments.explanation: The text in Section 5.1 states that 'the advanced clustering yields especially good performance over ppa10/ppa,' but Section 5.2 later says 'GNN-CM+... is shown to perform worse than GNN-CM overall' and 'Our advanced clustering... is able to achieve best results with GCN and GIN (with SAGE, all models perform rather bad over ppa10).' These statements are contradictory: the first claims advanced clustering is especially good on ppa10, while the second says it performs worse than GNN-CM overall and only achieves best results for two of three architectures. The paper should reconcile these claims by specifying which metric or setting is being referenced, or by removing the ambiguous 'especially good' phrasing.

comments.title: Notation error in assignment definition
comments.quote: define $\sigma(n):=\sigma^{\prime}
comments.explanation: The notation σ(n) is indeed a typo. The function σ was defined earlier as σ: V → [1,n], so its argument should be a graph node v ∈ V, not the integer n (which already denotes the number of virtual nodes). The intended definition is σ(v) := σ′(ρ(v)) for each v ∈ V. This is a clear notational inconsistency that should be corrected. Rewrite as: σ(v) := σ′(ρ(v)) for each graph node v ∈ V.

comments.title: Unclear handling of cluster count mismatch
comments.quote: . We choose a relatively large $m$, with $m\gg n$, and apply the above clustering algorithm, during initialization. Then, in each epoch, we randomly guess an assignment $\sigma^{\prime}:[1,m]\rightarrow[1,n]$ of clusters to
comments.explanation: The method GNN-CM+ uses m ≫ n clusters but only n virtual nodes, with a random mapping σ′ from clusters to virtual nodes. However, the paper does not specify how the clustering algorithm is run with m clusters when m is much larger than the number of nodes |V|. If m > |V|, the clustering algorithm would produce empty clusters, and the random assignment σ′ would map some clusters (possibly empty) to virtual nodes. This could lead to degenerate behavior where some virtual nodes receive no graph nodes. The authors should clarify that m ≤ |V| is required, or explain how empty clusters are handled. Add a remark after the quoted text: 'We assume m ≤ |V| to avoid empty clusters; if m > |V|, empty clusters are ignored.'

comments.title: Overclaiming theoretical novelty
comments.quote: We also advance research by providing theoretical justifications - the very first about applying virtual nodes at all - and by showing their positive impact in various experiments.
comments.explanation: The claim that this paper provides 'the very first' theoretical justifications about applying virtual nodes is overstated. Prior work on virtual nodes (e.g., Gilmer et al., 2017; Ishiguro et al., 2019) includes theoretical discussions, even if not as extensive. Moreover, the paper's own theoretical analysis in Section 4.3 has been flagged as flawed and incomplete (see overview issues). The claim should be tempered to reflect the actual contribution. Rewrite as 'We also advance research by providing theoretical justifications for virtual nodes in the context of link prediction, and by showing their positive impact in various experiments.'

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim about dense graphs
comments.quote: Future work includes the design of more advanced and scalable architectures, and it would be interesting to further investigate the huge performance increases on dense graphs.
comments.explanation: The phrase 'huge performance increases on dense graphs' is not supported by the experimental results presented in the paper. The paper's experiments focus on datasets like ogbl-collab, ogbl-ppa, and ogbl-ddi, which are not characterized as dense in the paper. No experiment explicitly isolates dense vs. sparse graphs, and no quantitative comparison of performance gains across graph densities is provided. This claim appears to be speculative and should be removed or replaced with a reference to specific datasets where large gains were observed. Remove 'huge performance increases on dense graphs' and instead write 'it would be interesting to investigate how performance varies with graph density.'
